inputs.prompt: Author: James Chen, CEO, Clearwater Analytics. Argument: Local government finance departments are making critical decisions with outdated tools. The private sector invested in AI forecasting years ago. Public money deserves at least the same level of analytical capability. Target publication: Public Finance Magazine (trade press for government finance professionals). Evidence: 1) Only 12% of local authorities use any form of AI in financial planning (internal survey). 2) £2.1 billion in local government overspend in the last fiscal year attributed to forecasting errors (NAO report). News hook: Government has just announced a new 'digital transformation' fund for local authorities.

Author: Helen Cross, Director, Riverside Arts Centre. Argument: Arts funding should not be treated as a luxury. Target publication: Local newspaper. Evidence: The arts centre brings £1.2M to the local economy annually.

Author: Dr. Ingrid Nørgaard, Executive Director, Nordic Health Initiative. Argument: Europe's healthcare systems are structured nationally but the diseases they fight do not respect borders. Cross-border healthcare research is not an aspiration — it is a necessity, and the EU must fund it as infrastructure, not as project grants. Target publication: Politico Europe. Evidence: 1) Nordic cross-border research collaborations produced 23% more published findings per euro invested than single-country projects (NHI internal data). 2) The EU4Health programme allocates less than 4% of its budget to cross-border research. News hook: EU budget negotiations for 2028-2034 are beginning.

Author: Carolina Vega, Founder, Open Rivers Alliance. Argument: Latin American governments are approving new hydroelectric dams on the basis of outdated environmental impact studies that ignore cumulative downstream effects. The region needs a basin-wide assessment standard before permitting any new dam. Target publication: Americas Quarterly. Evidence: 1) Fourteen new dam projects were approved across three Andean countries in the past two years, none with a cumulative impact assessment (Open Rivers Alliance monitoring data). 2) Fish migration routes on the Marañón basin have declined by 37% since the first dam was completed in 2019 (university research study). News hook: A consortium of international lenders has just announced financing for two additional dam projects.

inputs.description: Adversarial - controversial topic with weak evidence
inputs.prompt: Author: Mark Devlin, independent commentator. Argument: Remote work is destroying city centres and local governments should tax remote workers to compensate businesses that depend on commuter traffic. Target publication: National broadsheet opinion section. Evidence: 1) A coffee shop near my office closed last year. 2) I read somewhere that foot traffic is down 40% in city centres. 3) Other countries are thinking about this too.

Author: Dr. Amina Osei, Director of Policy, East African Education Forum. Argument: Kenya's competency-based curriculum reform is failing because it was designed in Nairobi boardrooms without input from rural teachers who must deliver it. The government must pause national rollout, fund teacher training in each county, and build an implementation timeline based on classroom readiness — not political deadlines. Target publication: The East African (regional newspaper of record). Evidence: 1) Only 22% of primary school teachers in rural counties have received the mandated CBC training (Education Forum survey of 1,400 teachers across 12 counties). 2) Textbook distribution for the new curriculum reached only 6 out of 47 counties by the start of term (Ministry of Education logistics data). 3) Student assessment scores in pilot counties showed no improvement after two years under CBC compared to the previous 8-4-4 system (independent evaluation by a regional research institute). News hook: The government has just announced it will extend CBC to secondary schools starting next year.

inputs.description: East Asian context - South Korean tech regulation
inputs.prompt: Author: Dr. Jae-won Park, Senior Fellow, Digital Policy Institute. Argument: South Korea's proposed AI transparency law would require companies to disclose training data sources and algorithmic decision-making logic, but the draft exempts national security applications — a loophole wide enough to swallow the entire regulation. Without closing this exemption, the law protects no one. Target publication: Nikkei Asia. Evidence: 1) 34% of AI procurement contracts issued by government agencies in the past fiscal year were classified under national security provisions and would be exempt from the proposed disclosure rules (DPI analysis of public procurement data). 2) Three consumer credit scoring companies have already restructured their AI operations under defence subsidiary shells to pre-emptively avoid compliance (investigative journalism by a Seoul-based media outlet). News hook: The National Assembly committee reviewing the bill is scheduled to vote next month.
